problem_summary: 2016 MCM/ICM Problem D (a network-science / information-flow problem). The team's interpretation: model how news/information spreads through society from the 1860s to today, characterize what makes a piece of information "important," predict how importance affects its spread across different network topologies and eras, and derive a strategy to optimize spread. Control #54740, Outstanding/O-award. It is operations-research/network-modeling in flavor: graph topology, diffusion dynamics, and an optimal-seeding question.
models_used: Enhanced compartmental diffusion ODE with multiple topical communities: a discretized update Δy = R(t){Σ_i Σ_{j≠i} [β_i y_i(t) + η_ij y_j(t)](N_i − y_i(t))} Δt, where communities are politics/entertainment/consumption, β_i is intra-community spread rate, η_ij is cross-community leak rate, with the ordered constraint β_j < η_ji < η_ij < β_i

Time-varying external Relevance Factor R(t) scaled 1–10, built from four sub-attributes (Novelty/Shock, Accessibility, Attractiveness, Compatibility); R(t) rises and decays exponentially with speed proportional to relevance, controlling penetration, velocity, and retention

Discrete-time agent-based network model on a directed graph: node internal state T_{0,t} = Π_k(1 − I_{0,t,k}) with absorption I_{0,t,k} = n_{k,t−1}·P_{N_0}, and pulsed external broadcast n_{0,t} = δ_{t,t0+G}·P_{S_0}·I_{0,t} using a Kronecker-delta gestation period G — described by the team as a modified Independent Cascade / SIR hybrid

Baseline literature models invoked and extended: classic technology-diffusion ODE dA/dt = i(t)[P − A(t)] with the three Geroski diffusion-coefficient forms (α, βA(t), α+βA(t)); Granovetter Threshold model and Kempe-Kleinberg-Tardos Independent Cascade model as the two prior graph paradigms

'Ghost node' construct for edge latency: auxiliary nodes with P_S=P_N=1 and gestation v−1 to give an edge a transmission speed of v steps (models e.g. newspapers traveling by train), enabling directed/undirected and variable-distance edges on one graph

Memory/forgetting decay: I_{0,t} = I_{0,t0}·P_{V_{0,t}} with a time-dependent weighted probability that pushes nodes back toward 'forgetting' as t − t0 grows, allowing re-infection from a second source

Exponential-decay curve fitting of real archival mention-volume data (least-squares fits reported with R^2: Berlin 1.00, Lincoln 0.96, WS 0.94, Suffrage 0.90, Marilyn Monroe 0.89) used to validate the R(t) decay shape
Two-source competition model with 50/50 contention rule at contested nodes, used for sensitivity analysis over relevance imbalance, initial-node connectivity, and edge-distance/isolation
key_techniques: Decomposing a vague qualitative prompt ('importance') into a quantified, named composite: the 1–10 Relevance Factor = f(Novelty, Accessibility, Attractiveness, Compatibility), giving the whole paper a single tunable knob

Two-stage threshold definition of 'news' (threshold of penetration = min mentions to catch wind; threshold of retention = min relevance duration) to filter signal from background noise before modeling

Layering models rather than choosing one: a macro continuous diffusion ODE for aggregate volume PLUS a micro discrete agent/graph simulator for spatial topology — the two are cross-checked against the same real data

Empirical grounding via historical text-mining: tracked phrase mention-volume in Chronicling America (10M+ newspapers, 1836–1922) and the Washington Post (1960–63) for Lincoln, Suffrage, World Series, Marilyn Monroe, Berlin — then fit and reported R^2

Era-specific parameterization: topical node proportions seeded from real circulation/traffic data (1880 ≈ 89% politics/1% entertainment vs 2015 ≈ 48% entertainment/28% politics/24% consumption), letting the same engine represent different centuries

Cross-era robustness test (a clever validation trick): run modern news (Osama bin Laden relevance 10, Kim Kardashian relevance 6) through the 1880 network and compare to historical Lincoln-assassination vs inaugural-ball curves

Graph-theoretic encoding of physical/communication realities: directed edges = consumption media (newspaper/radio/TV), undirected = upload media (internet/telegraph/talking), edge velocity = geographic distance, ghost nodes = latency

Building and deploying a live interactive web simulator (hosted at an EC2 IP in the appendix) that animates the cascade, supports random or controlled single-event injection and dual-source competition, and exports tabular data
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on (1) interpretation depth — it took a deliberately open, fuzzy social-science prompt and imposed crisp, defensible structure (named categories, a single 1–10 Relevance Factor decomposed into four attributes, two explicit thresholds for what counts as 'news'); (2) the rare combination of a clean analytic model and a working simulator, with both validated against REAL historical data, not just toy curves — text-mining millions of archived newspapers and reporting R^2 values is exactly the empirical seriousness judges reward; (3) genuine modeling creativity that stays rigorous — the 'ghost node' trick to encode edge latency and the directed-vs-undirected edge semantics show the team could bend standard Independent-Cascade/SIR machinery to fit messy reality; (4) a memorable validation narrative (Lincoln's assassination, Berlin Wall, Marilyn Monroe, bin Laden vs Gaddafi) that makes abstract dynamics concrete and ties the model to events a reader already knows; and (5) the cross-era stress test (running 2011/2015 news through an 1880 network) demonstrates the model's generality in a way that reads as confident rather than hand-wavy. It connects every modeling choice back to the data and back to the question."
section_highlights: "Summary: leads with a clear conceptual frame (consumption/politics/entertainment + relevance factor) and previews concrete validated examples (Lincoln, Berlin Wall) — judge knows the punchline immediately. Section 2 'What is news?': turns the prompt's ambiguity into operational definitions (penetration/retention thresholds) before any math — strong altitude control. Section 3 builds models in escalating layers (prior-art ODE → multi-community diffusion → discrete graph → ghost-node/forgetting extensions), each motivated by a named limitation of the last. Figures 8–9: explicit exponential fits with an R^2 table — quantitative validation presented honestly. Section 4 validity + 4.2 robustness: real-vs-model overlays (Marilyn Monroe, Alan Rickman) and the cross-era test. Section 6 sensitivity: cleanly factored into importance imbalance / initial connectivity / connection distance (island-vs-mainland isolation), each with a physically-intuitive interpretation. Section 7.1: candid, specific strengths AND weaknesses (random connections, static graph, discrete time). Appendix: link to a live hosted simulator."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Randomly generated graph connectivity — the model captures community-level topical proportions but NOT homophily (clustering of like-minded people), which the team admits washes out real individual preference structure
Static graph: no nodes/edges are added, removed, or rewired over a run, and the macro-level era shift (1880 vs 2015) is handled by re-seeding parameters rather than modeling endogenous network growth

Heavy reliance on weighted-random share/notice probabilities (P_N, P_S, P_V) and a flat 50/50 contention rule at contested nodes — these are asserted, not estimated from data, so parameter choices are somewhat arbitrary

Validation is largely visual curve-matching plus a handful of single-phrase R^2 fits; the model is tuned to reproduce a few famous spikes, raising overfitting/cherry-picking concern and no held-out or quantitative error metric across many events

Some embedded data tables in the markdown are clearly garbled/placeholder (e.g. the bin Laden/Gaddafi 'mentions' column shows identical ~3.16e17 values, and several mention counts look schematic), so the published figures should be read as illustrative not exact

The four Relevance-Factor sub-attributes (Novelty, Accessibility, Attractiveness, Compatibility) are never operationalized into a measurable formula — relevance values like '9' or '10' are assigned by judgment, weakening predictive claims

Strong simplifying assumptions stated up front (uniform topical distribution within an era; a node shares with ALL neighbors when it shares; one information string per node at a time) limit realism for dense modern networks
reusable_patterns: The 'composite index' move: when a prompt asks about a fuzzy quality (importance, resilience, attractiveness), define it as a single named score on a fixed scale (here 1–10) decomposed into a small set of labeled sub-attributes — gives the model one clean control variable and a clear narrative spine
Pair a continuous aggregate model with a discrete agent/network simulator and validate BOTH against the same real dataset — judges reward the macro/micro cross-check
Pre-filter what 'counts' with explicit thresholds (penetration + retention) before modeling dynamics — cleanly separates signal from noise and pre-empts scope objections
Ground a social/historical model in mineable archives (Chronicling America, newspaper/Google Trends volume) and report fit quality (R^2) — empirical grounding beats synthetic-only validation
Cross-regime robustness test: run inputs from one regime through a model parameterized for another (modern news in an 1880 network) to demonstrate generality without new data

Encode physical/temporal realities directly into graph structure: directed vs undirected edges for media type, edge weights/velocities for distance, and auxiliary 'ghost' nodes to inject latency into an otherwise one-step cascade
Ship a working, hosted interactive simulator and reference it in the appendix — concrete evidence the model runs, plus a memorable artifact for judges

Factor the sensitivity analysis along clearly separated, physically-meaningful axes (relevance imbalance, seed connectivity, geographic isolation) each with an intuitive real-world analogy (rural village vs city, island vs mainland)
